speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number two of the interview with Marianna Glazek. You were just, actually, talking about your first husband. What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was George. And we knew each other for-- for many years. And then, finally, my father agreed, in 1942, that we should get married. Then there wasn't really too much choice. And we moved into our own apartment. But we only lived together like eight months or nine months, because then they called him into forced labor camp. [COUGHS] Excuse me. And he was called into-- into a camp that was in Budapest, actually. But he couldn't go home unless he-- he got special permission. But he just tried to escape home. So for a few months, that's what we did. He came home once in a while, and-- and then he went away again. And I never knew when I'm going to see him again. And I went with my parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: So those last two years-- '42 and forty-- starting with '42, after we got married in August, then '43 was pretty bad. I mean, we were waiting from one coming home to the other, and heard all those terrible news about people in the country who were-- who were deported.

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, we did not realize how terrible it was. And I still have a hard time explaining to myself how come we didn't realize that they were actually deported, that they weren't going to come home at all. And they were-- they could not send us regular mail. So there was a kind of pre-printed card, which they wrote their name and said, we're feeling very well. We're feeling wonderful. We are on our way to a-- to a camp. And we're going to write you later. So we were in this kind of a mood about-- everything is going to be all right. Everything is going to be all right. But we are still worried about it. Then I hid-- I got pregnant. And my father said I shouldn't have the baby. This was not a time to have a baby. And actually, I-- I got rid-- I had a-- I got rid of the baby. Then I got pregnant again. And at that time, I-- I had the baby in 1944, November 26th-- 26, 27. And-- and I kept it. But that-- that was the time when we already moved out of our apartment. In 1943-- '43, '44, 1944, we first had to turn in all our possessions. There was a big sign appeared on the street that Jews have to turn in their bicycles. That was the hardest thing of all, because we used the bicycle a lot. We had to turn in the bicycles, all the jewelry, all the silver, and all the China, and all the valuable things that were not turned in up to that time. And we had to take it ourselves and turn it in. Then there came another law, in which they  said that Jews should move into designated housing, into Jewish housing. At that time, several-- three of the countries took the responsibility upon themselves to designate certain houses as their house, as their own house. And we moved into a house that was claimed by the Swedish government as being under their protection. And it happened to be the house that my sister-in-law and her husband and her mother lived, in an apartment that wasn't very far from us. But at that time, my-- my brother-in-law was already gone to a forced labor camp. He never came back. This was my brother-in-law, and he was an executive for Shell Oil Company. But he was Hungarian. And so his-- his wife-- my husband's brother-- he was much younger than she was. And-- and her mother and his mother, they lived there. So we decided that that's going to be the best place for us to move. And we moved into that house. But we couldn't-- in the meantime, our apartment was confiscated by the Germans. It was a very nice German officer who didn't mind for us to be there at all. But we could not live there anymore. My father was allowed for a while to go in during office hours and have his office there. I am not even sure where the Germans lived, but they lived in our apartment. And pretty soon then, my father couldn't go out anymore, because there were certain hours when you were-- when you were allowed to leave, to go out of the house. We had to wear a star, a yellow star designating us as-- as Jews. Everybody had to sew it on. It must have been in March when the Germans-- in '44, when the Germans moved into Hungary and took up positions. And-- and that was a time that we realized that it's going to come pretty soon to Budapest. So we are not those privileged people who escape all that. And so we moved in with my mother-in-law. We didn't get along very well. We never did get along very well with her. And all her things were there. And we used all her thing. And we didn't have our own sheets, and our own pillow cases, and our own beddings. But we-- all of us moved, like three-- three or four of us moved into a small room, where she stayed with my-- In the meantime, my sister actually left, too. She was-- she was hiding somewhere. We didn't know where. At one point she disappeared, and my father and I took off the stars, and were looking for her all day long. That was in summer-- must have been July or August. We're looking for her all-- all day long, and then it turned out that she went with-- to yoga teacher. At that time when nobody in Budapest even knew how to spell yoga, she knew-- she exercised, and she did it. She was a very good gymnast. She-- in the school try-outs, she was always the first doing gymnastics. And at the school's festival, at the end of every year, when we of had performances, and so on, she was always the biggest drawing card for-- she was so good in gymnastics, that, actually, she was supposed to go to the Olympics. But then she couldn't, because she was Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she did not live with us. She lived off and on, I think. And that was-- that must have been in July-- must have been in August, July or August. So at one point, we decided that it's not going to take very long anymore. And the Germans, evidently, did not live in the apartment. So we decided we're going to go to our apartment and hide out there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father took us all. And my-- my sister came first with us. But then she decided that she would only come if her boyfriend could come, too. And my father didn't want that. So I don't think she came with us. But she came to visit. And a-- a maid what we had before-- brought every day our food. And she really liked to came, and was very friendly and so on. But then finally, it turned out that she was the one who-- who talked about us, and told people that we are there. And we had to move out. We were for two weeks-- it was two or three weeks we were hiding in the apartment. And we couldn't talk. It was very much like Anne Frank's story. We couldn't talk loudly enough. I was-- I remember the books I was reading. I was reading by [? Thomas ?] [? Martin ?].

speaker: interviewee
text: I was reading and [? and [? it was a trilogy, three books. I haven't thought of that for a long time, so I actually forgot it. I was reading all day long. I was reading all the time. And we couldn't talk about the books, because we weren't supposed to talk. We were afraid that the neighbors will realize that we are there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then at one point, we moved back into the-- our-- the apartment. That was an apartment, a Swedish house that was protected by Wallenberg's agency. And I think the reason for that was that that maid who brought us the food, she told somebody about us. And we could not stay there anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you were hiding, you had your daughter with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No she wasn't born yet. I had her with me, but she wasn't born. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Then she probably didn't cry much.

speaker: interviewee
text: I just remember from that time that my father used to bring all the food home for me. I was the one, because I had to eat. Nobody else had to eat, but I had to eat. I had green peppers for breakfast. That was considered to be very healthy, because it has vitamin C, since that was the time when the Hungarian scientist, Dr. Szent-Gyorgyi won a Nobel Prize for finding that there were-- that there was vitamin C in the green pepper. So I was the one who ate all the good food, and everybody else did not eat so well. Excuse me. [DRINKS] It's getting to be drinkable now. [DRINKS] [INAUDIBLE] And everywhere, everything that-- Hungarian sausage is also very important in the Hungarian diet. So I was the one who got that food, always.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we had to move back. And that must have been like August, September and so on. We had to move back into the apartment, and [INAUDIBLE] where my sister-in-law and my mother-in-law lived.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father was always taking care of me. He was cutting my toenails, and all the other thing, and worrying how I'm going to deliver the baby. He was-- he was very worried. But he had all his [INAUDIBLE] friends, and there was a very famous gynecologist obstetrician in the Jewish hospital. And he talked to him on the phone. That was the time my father couldn't go out anymore. He couldn't go out to take care of his patients, or he couldn't go out to take care of any new patients. So he didn't really use his office much anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he told me that Dr. [NON-ENGLISH] I think was his name-- he's going to take care of me when I have the baby. It just so happened that the doctor was extremely heavy. I mean, just very heavy, like 400 pounds or something, 300 pounds, something like this. He told me not to get scared because he's a very good doctor in spite of the fact that he is so heavy. So we-- we are coming-- we're in the year-- to November. We were actually in November. And it was around November, the beginning of December, when the Russians-- the Russians came close, approached Budapest, and were trying to cut off the-- the routes that were going to Budapest.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody was scared. There was no food. It was very hard to get hold of food. [DRINKS] Everybody was-- was thinking of what's going to happen. And my sister-in-law, in the meantime, went away somewhere, to hide somewhere. And my sister was also gone. And she told us that she's hiding, and not to look for her. Actually, we were once, already, as I said that in the other tape-- we were also, we were looking-- my father and I were looking for her for a whole day. Then finally, we found her somewhere in-- in the country. That was a pretty dangerous kind of trip for us. And we were trying to find-- find her. And finally, we found her. And then she said that she's leaving. And if we won't take her boyfriend, then she's not going to stay with us, something to that effect. So there was only my-- my mother, my father, and myself who were there in the apartment. And by Christmas Eve-- Christmas Eve-- the city was surrounded by Russians. And nobody could get out or get in.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we didn't really know what was going on. Only afterwards we found out that there was this American, this girl from Israel, who was a Hungarian girl, but she lived in Israel, who was apprehended. And what was her name? Remember her name? Hannah Senesh.

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Hannah Senesh.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hannah Senesh, who was actually right across from where we stayed-- we lived. We lived near the Danube, and she was on the-- on the other side of the Danube in the castle, in the-- in the jail. And she was executed. That, I just found out when I came to the United States. And so the town was surrounded. And there was nothing that we could do. We couldn't get out. We couldn't get in. We had to live just the best way we could. My sister-in-law was gone. My sister was gone. There was only my-- my mother-in-law and the three of us left. And then at Christmas Eve-- in November, actually, something happened. And that was my daughter was born. That was November the 26th. That was before the town was-- it was surrounded. It was almost surrounded, but that was before it was completely surrounded. And so my father couldn't come with us. So he called the first aid. And they picked me up and took me to the hospital. Now, that was an experience in itself, because in the hospital, when we called, they said they had no place. I can't come, because most of the places are taken by people who tried to commit suicide. [DRINKS] And actually that hospital was for women to give birth. But they said they have no place. The only way I can come-- if I-- if I bring my own bedding. And I told them I have no bedding, because I'm not living at home. And I don't have anything-- anything there. Well, they says I can't come then. But I went, anyway. And I took some sheets and pillowcases from my mother-in-law. And it turned out, when I got there, they put me and another woman in one bed. Two women and two babies in one bed. And she was born the first night. I remember the doctor was, like, 400 pounds, 300 pounds, 400 pounds. And he jumped on me and pressed out the afterbirth. This was a pretty drastic way of doing it. But that's the way he did it. And I was crying all the time when I was there. The hospital was in such bad shape. There was water in the rooms where-- where people were sleeping in the bed. There water up to the ankles because the sewers didn't work. And the babies didn't get their own place. They had to sleep in the same bed where the mothers were sleeping. And they didn't bring them any food. And if you didn't have milk, it was just tough luck. And things were just really very bad. Once during the time I was there, my husband came in to visit me. He was dressed up like an old man. And he was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] had a stick, and pretended that he was not able to walk. And he came to visit me. But it turned out that it was him after a while. And he came to visit me, and I was very happy that he came. But he said that-- he saw the baby, and he said, well, he thought it's going to be nicer-looking. But he hoped that the baby would be able to play the piano well. [DRINKS] In the meantime-- she doesn't play the piano. But that was OK, because he-- my husband loved music very much. And he played just from hearing. He loved Beethoven's Ninth Symphony. And he kind of made up his mind that maybe his daughter won't be beautiful, but she will be able to play the piano pretty well.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I stayed there, maybe, a week. But I still have some of the letters that he wrote me. And my mother sent, every day, something for me to eat. I mean, you didn't get anything to eat in the hospital. So somebody had to send you something to eat. And having your first baby under these circumstances was not exactly considered to be very ideal, but that was the only thing I could do. So I-- I took this for granted, cried most of the time, but still accepted it. And then I went home. And I can't remember anymore who picked me up and how I got home. But finally, I got home with the baby. She was all right. And then we waited for anything that's going to happen. So on Christmas Eve, it was Christmas Eve when we found out that the town, the city, is surrounded-- is surrounded by Russians. And at that time, we thought the Russians were like the angels. They came to save us. That they were our savior. Even when I was there last year, I told them. And they weren't very happy about it, [DRINKS] because the Russians were already gone last-- when they were there last year-- no. Two years ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Russians were already gone. And they decided that the Russians didn't save the city, but they took the city. So they tried to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to make the Russians look pretty bad. But actually, they were the ones who saved us. And when I went home, it was very hard, because I didn't have any milk. I didn't think I had any milk. There was no way to get any milk. That was on Christmas Eve, Christmas Eve the 24th. On December the 30th, in the morning, as it was our habit, we went down to the bunkers, because the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think it was the British. We thought it was the Americans-- came and bombed the city every day. Every day we went down to the bunker. And there was no difference if you were-- most of the people were Jewish there because it was a Jewish house. But we really didn't know each other very well.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we came on down in the morning. And around 11 o'clock, they told us to come upstairs. And it turned out that there were the Hungarian Nazis there. They were ready to pick us up and take us. There was no other way. I couldn't even go up to get some diapers.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother and I had the baby in a basket, in a bushel basket. That was like-- the bushel baskets were long, and were really baskets, not plastic. And we had her-- we had her there. And they made us stand in line. And then they took us out, and in front of the house, made us stand in front of the house. And we were walking-- we were walking down the street to the house where the Hungarian Nazis had their headquarters. They called that Freedom House, I think. And we-- and we were taken to the Freedom House, and taken to the Nazis, and were asked questions. I know that they took my watch away, my rings away. They said I don't need the rings anymore. And they had all kind of things that they were doing to people. And when-- then in the evening-- they didn't give us any food, of course-- but the evening, they said we should stand in line. They said that 80 people, 80 of the men, should go in the front. And my father was always very law-abiding, so he went in front. Well, my husband didn't go in front. He stayed with us. [DRINKS] And that was the most terrible part, this. But the Hungarian Nazis, they were uneducated, young people who didn't know what was going on. They were just left to do whatever they wanted. And they were terrorizing people. And they said we're all going to go now to the ghetto, I think they said. But they made everybody undress, just have their underwear and something. This was December the 30th, the coldest night of the year, probably. And my mother and I were allowed to keep our clothes on because we were carrying the bushel basket. One person couldn't carry the bushel basket. Two people had to carry the bushel basket. And so we were standing in line. And my-- my father was in the front. We assembled in the courtyard. And I remember my father said, poor Eva, she will come home and won't find anybody anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that was his last words. And he gave me his handkerchief, that I still have, and some other things. And we said goodbye to each other. And he went in front. And then we all started the march. We went through town. From the house we lived to the house on [NON-ENGLISH] was not very far. It was during the day.

speaker: interviewee
text: But this was at night, and this was going to the Danube. And it got colder and colder if we came closer to the Danube. Then we went in front of the parliament. And there was a young man who-- who-- and his father. And they tried to escape or something. There was some kind of things going on, because one of the Nazis took his gun and shot the father and shot the son. They were trying to-- they said they were trying to escape. So actually, I saw that. And then we were standing-- they made us stand in front of the parliament. It was-- every time I see the parliament, I remember that. And they cut off the 80 men in front. And we only heard them firing their gun. And we did not, of course, believe that they shot them in the Danube. You never believe the worst things that can happen. But they did shoot them at that time, into the Danube. And some of them escaped by trying to swim. Some of them escaped by trying to appear like they were going to swim. And most of them didn't. Most of them were killed right there. And that was the last time, of course, I saw my father. And the next day-- then we stood there for a while. And then, some police came dressed in police uniform and talked to us.
